paragraphs: Corinth was the capital of Achaia, a very rich and populous city, where St. Paul had preached a year and a half, and converted a great many. See Acts 18:10. Now having received a letter from them, (chap. 7:1.) and being informed of divers disputes and divisions among them, (chap. 1:11.) he wrote this letter to them, and sent it by the same persons, Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus, who had brought him their letter, chap. 16:17. It was written about the year 56, not from Philippi, as it is commonly marked at the end of the Greek copies, but rather from Ephesus. The subject and main design of this Epistle was to take away the divisions among them about the talents and merits of those who had baptized and preached to them, and to settle divers matters of ecclesiastical discipline. The apostle justifieth his mission, and his manner of preaching, chap. 1, 2, 3, and 4. He teacheth them what was to be done with the man guilty of a scandalous sin of incest, chap. 5. He speaks of sins against chastity; of matrimony; and of the state of continency, chap. 6 and 7. Of meats offered to idols, chap. 8. Of his manner of conversing with them, and what their conversation ought to be, chap. 9. and 10. Of the holy sacrament of the Eucharist, chap. 11. Of the different gifts of the Holy Ghost, and how to employ them, chap. 12, 13, and 14. Of the faith of the resurrection, chap. 15. Of charitable contributions, and of his design of coming again to them, chap. 16. (Witham) --- St. Paul having planted the faith in Corinth, where he had preached a year and a half, and converted a great many, went to Ephesus. After being there three years, he wrote this first Epistle to the Corinthians, and sent it by the same persons, Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus, who had brought their letter to him. It was written about twenty-four years after our Lord's ascension, and contains several matters appertaining to faith and morals, and also to ecclesiastical discipline. (Challoner)
